_comment: Argent Mesh — LAN P2P coordination between the machines running Argent Utils. Protocol constants, the duty catalog (which job classes the mesh routes), and the placement strategies. Consumed by the Python mesh node (linux/argent_utils/mesh) today; a future Swift node reads the same file. Node-local attributes (tier, token state) live in ~/.argent/mesh/node.json on each machine; mesh-wide placement overrides are gossiped last-writer-wins and start from the defaults here.

protocol._comment: Discovery is UDP (multicast + subnet broadcast, receivers dedupe — Wi-Fi APs commonly drop multicast); links are TCP with NDJSON messages. A node is 'stale' after missing heartbeats for peerStaleSecs and 'down' (duties reassigned) after peerTimeoutSecs.

trust._comment: How much a node trusts a PEER. Trust is NEVER derived from an advertised field (those are spoofable). Each device has an Ed25519 keypair; a peer must sign a fresh per-connection challenge to prove it holds the key it advertises, and trust keys on that verified key's fingerprint against a LOCAL, operator-managed allowlist (~/.argent/mesh/trusted.json), never gossiped. 'personal' = the peer's proven fingerprint is in my allowlist → a received SzpontRequest runs directly, as if I'd triggered it locally. 'foreign' = a device I have not marked personal → its requests are declined by default, or — with a confinement runner (ARGENT_MESH_FOREIGN_SPAWN) configured — run confined and response-only: the compute runs sandboxed and the result is routed back for the requester to act on (never a social action here). See docs/szpontnet/13-foreign-execution.md. 'default' is the trust level for an UNKNOWN device (not in the allowlist, or unverified/keyless): it ships 'foreign' (zero-trust by default — a new device is untrusted until the operator promotes it to personal), and can be overridden per-node (ARGENT_MESH_DEFAULT_TRUST, or the panel's default-trust toggle, persisted in trusted.json). Set it to 'personal' to restore the pre-trust full-altruism mode where every unlisted peer is trusted.

accounts._comment: Subscription plans and the accounting knobs behind per-node load balancing. A plan's 'weight' is its quota capacity RELATIVE to Pro (Max 5× → 5, Max 20× → 20); capacity units = weight × capacityPerWeight. Absolute token quotas are intentionally NOT hard-coded — Anthropic's limits are dynamic rolling windows — so surplus is compared in these relative units. usageAvg is an exponential rolling average of consumption with a ~21-day time constant; quotaLeft is remaining capacity in the current window. A node's dispatch surplus = quotaLeft − usageAvg (see dispatchStrategy).
accounts._tokenComment: Automatic token-budget signal (the ok/low/out state the panel shows and the mesh routes around). PRIMARY: the node probes the account's REAL remaining quota via the OAuth usage endpoint (the same data Claude Code's /usage shows) — utilization of the 5-hour session and 7-day week rate-limit windows, gossiped as tokensSessionPct/tokensWeekPct; the binding (smaller) fraction drives the state: ≥ lowThreshold → ok, > 0 → low, ≤ 0 → out. FALLBACK (no credentials / offline / ARGENT_MESH_OAUTH_PROBE=0): the node measures its OWN recent consumption from the local Claude logs (~/.claude/projects/**/*.jsonl, input+output+cache-creation tokens over the last usageWindowHours) against a heuristic per-plan ceiling = plan.weight × tokensPerWeight. The fallback is deliberately rough (real limits are dynamic and account-specific) — the UIs mark it '≈'; tune tokensPerWeight, or pin a node's state with the manual override.

tiers._comment: Machine strength, 1 = strongest. 'weakest-first' keeps the strong machine free for interactive work by routing grunt jobs to the weakest eligible node. Auto-detected CPU-quality-first (Apple-Silicon variant / boost clock, then physical cores, RAM, dGPU — see mesh/hardware.py); a manual edit in the panel pins it. 'labels' give the UIs human words instead of a bare number.

strategies.title: Weakest first
strategies.detail: route to the weakest eligible machine — keeps the strong ones free

strategies.title: Strongest first
strategies.detail: route to the strongest eligible machine — fastest wall-clock

strategies.title: Local first
strategies.detail: run on the dispatching machine when eligible, else weakest-first

strategies.title: Most surplus first
strategies.detail: route to the node with the most spare quota (quotaLeft − usageAvg), account-type aware; ties fall back to weakest-first

_dispatchStrategyComment: The ranking a DISPATCHER uses to pick a target when it dispatches a SzpontRequest — the load-balancing decision, made unilaterally by the dispatching node from its own gossiped view (no consensus). Separate from a duty's placement 'strategy', which drives the stable displayed ownership. Defaults to surplus-first so requests flow to whoever has the most spare quota.

placement._comment: The bundle E2E runs on one Linux AND one macOS node — platform coverage beats machine preference here. An empty spread means 'any one node'.
